introSection: Shockwave was a steel stand-up roller coaster located at Kings Dominion in Doswell, Virginia, USA. It was manufactured by TOGO. The ride operated from 1986 to 2015.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
After the closure of Galaxi in 1983, Kings Dominion hired TOGO to build a compact stand-up coaster. Shockwave opened on March 23, 1986 at a cost of $4 million.

The ride was repainted in 2000 in coordination with the park's 25th anniversary.

Following Togo's bankruptcy in 2001, replacement parts for Shockwave were harder to find, and the ride's lines were shrinking because of other attractions such as Intimidator 305, Dominator, and, at the time, Volcano The Blast Coaster. On July 9, 2015, Kings Dominion announced that Shockwave would close permanently on August 9, 2015 after serving over 22 million riders. On August 20, 2015, Kings Dominion announced that Shockwave would be replaced in 2016 by Delirium, a Mondial frisbee ride. The ride was demolished for scrap metal in November 2015. Shockwave was the last of three Togo stand-up roller coasters built in the North America to close, and is also the last Togo coaster in the U.S. to close, leaving just one Togo roller coaster in the U.S. left in operation, which is the Big Apple Coaster, a hypercoaster located at New York, New York Hotel & Casino in Las Vegas, Nevada.

One of the ride's cars is now on display at the National Roller Coaster Museum in Plainview, Texas, USA.

sections.title: Design
sections.content: The ride had a vertical loop, the ride's only inversion, followed by a rise into a downward helix. Two airtime hills, separated by a 180 degree turn followed. The ride was very similar to King Cobra, which opened at Kings Island two years prior to Shockwave.

sections.title: Color scheme
sections.content: Green track and turquoise supports. The ride opened with blue supports and track, with the rails being yellow.

sections.title: Incident
sections.content: On August 23, 1999, a 20-year-old man was killed after he fell from Shockwave. An investigation found that he had partially but intentionally taken off his restraint, and his disregard of the safety rules prompted his death. A further investigation found the restraints to be working properly.

fullText:
Shockwave was a steel stand-up roller coaster located at Kings Dominion in Doswell, Virginia, USA. It was manufactured by TOGO. The ride operated from 1986 to 2015.

After the closure of Galaxi in 1983, Kings Dominion hired TOGO to build a compact stand-up coaster. Shockwave opened on March 23, 1986 at a cost of $4 million.

The ride was repainted in 2000 in coordination with the park's 25th anniversary.

Following Togo's bankruptcy in 2001, replacement parts for Shockwave were harder to find, and the ride's lines were shrinking because of other attractions such as Intimidator 305, Dominator, and, at the time, Volcano The Blast Coaster. On July 9, 2015, Kings Dominion announced that Shockwave would close permanently on August 9, 2015 after serving over 22 million riders. On August 20, 2015, Kings Dominion announced that Shockwave would be replaced in 2016 by Delirium, a Mondial frisbee ride. The ride was demolished for scrap metal in November 2015. Shockwave was the last of three Togo stand-up roller coasters built in the North America to close, and is also the last Togo coaster in the U.S. to close, leaving just one Togo roller coaster in the U.S. left in operation, which is the Big Apple Coaster, a hypercoaster located at New York, New York Hotel & Casino in Las Vegas, Nevada.

One of the ride's cars is now on display at the National Roller Coaster Museum in Plainview, Texas, USA.

The ride had a vertical loop, the ride's only inversion, followed by a rise into a downward helix. Two airtime hills, separated by a 180 degree turn followed. The ride was very similar to King Cobra, which opened at Kings Island two years prior to Shockwave.

Green track and turquoise supports. The ride opened with blue supports and track, with the rails being yellow.



On August 23, 1999, a 20-year-old man was killed after he fell from Shockwave. An investigation found that he had partially but intentionally taken off his restraint, and his disregard of the safety rules prompted his death. A further investigation found the restraints to be working properly.
